title: 史溫侯的福爾摩沙自然史
alternative: Swinhoe on the natural history of Formosa
description: 本文為他人撰述史溫侯作為福爾摩沙自然史研究權威，包含地理環境、18種哺乳動物、201種鳥類、15種爬蟲類、246種植物，另外也提及史溫侯聘僱了30名隨從專門負責採集鳥類標本的工作，另外6名隨從則是花了好幾個月製作剝製鳥類標本，

relation.name: Reed Digital Collections:Formosa Nineteenth Century Images

moreInfo.title: 來源
moreInfo.description: Anon.  "Swinhoe on the natural history of Formosa."  The Natural History Review (Belfast Natural History and Philosophical Society, Cork Cuvierian Society, Dublin Natural History Society) 4, xiii (January 1864): 31-37.（不具名。〈史溫侯的福爾摩沙自然史〉。《自然史評論》（貝爾法斯特自然史暨哲學學會、科克・庫維利安學會、都柏林自然史學會）第4輯卷8（1864年1月）第31-37頁。）

languageDocuments.text:
##--SPLIT--##V.—SWINHOE ON THE NATURAL HISTORY O_ FORMASA(1.) NOTES ON THE ISLAND OF FORMOSA. By Robert Swinhoe, F.G.S., F.Z.S., and H.M. Consul at Taiwan. London, 1863. Privately printed. (2.) NOTES ON THE ETHNOLOGY OF FORMOSA. By Robert Swinhoe, &c. London, 1863. (Privately printed.)(3.) ON THE MAMMALS OF THE ISLAND OF FORMOSA. By Robert Swinhoe, &c. Proc. Zool. Soc. 1862, p. 347. (4.) THE ORNITHOLOGY OF FORMOSA OR TAIWAN. By Robert Swinhoe, &c. The Ibis, 1863, pp. 198, 250, 377. (5.) A LIST OF FORMOSAN REPTILES, with notes on a few of the Species, and some Remarks on a Fish (Orthagoriscus, sp.). By Robert Swinhoe. Ann. and Mag. N.H. 1863. (6.) LIST OF PLANTS FROM THE ISLAND of FORMOSA OR TAIWAN. By R. Swinhoe. (Privately printed.) Two years ago, the great island of Formosa, opposite the Southern coast of China, was a terra incognita to naturalists, and nearly so to geographers. In December, 1860, Mr. Robert Swinhoe, of Her Majesty's Consular Service, was appointed to the new post of Vice-##--SPLIT--##consul at Taiwan-foe—the chief Chinese port of the island. A reference to Mr. Swinhoe's various papers—a list of which is given above —will show that in the very short time that has elapsed since his appointment, Mr. Swinhoe has already made considerable advances towards turning our former ignorance of Formosa and its inhabitants into a tolerably complete knowledge of this subject, and as this gentleman has again returned, after the expiration of a short term of absence, to his official duties, he will, no doubt, continue his exertions, and perfect the task he has so worthily commenced.     Formosa, as we learn from Mr. Swinhoe's writings, for he is almost our only authority on the subject, lies nearly due north and south off the coast of China, about 80 miles from the mainland. The island is about 120 miles in length, and varies from 26 to 80 in breadth, its entire area being about equal to that of Ireland. One-third of it consists of level land, the rest of undulated country and a mountainous ridge, some of the peaks of which attain a height of no less than 12,000 feet, and are capped with perpetual snow. In geographical conformation, therefore, Formosa is very different from the island to which we have likened it as regards size. And still more different is it as regards its Zoology; for whereas Ireland, having been recently divided from Europe, contains few, if any, species not met with also in the adjoining continent, Formosa, as we shall presently show, having been separated from the mainland for a series of ages, has undergone a remarkable change in its zoology, and has become the possessor of some very easily distinguishable representative forms.     To begin with a few words on the Ethnology of Formosa, this island and its dependencies are peopled, broadly speaking, by two races of men-aboriginal savages of the Malay type, and Chinese colonists of the Mongol type. The Chinese occupy the plains on the western side of the island, opposite the Chinese coast; the aborigines are confined to the hills on the eastern half of the island. Of the latter, which alone are of any ethnological interest, there appear to be five tribes, the Kalees, of the southern mountains; the Kweiyings, of the northern mountains; the Tylokoks, of the north-east mountains; the Komalans, of the north-east plains; and the Pepos, of Sinkang. The last are almost entirely amalgamated with the Chinese, and the last but one is fast disappearing. A sixth tribe, the people of Sakkam, seem to be quite extinct. Mr. Swinhoe's paper gives all the information he has been able to acquire concerning these tribes, which are probably erratic Malays of the same stock as the     ##--SPLIT--##原件缺第33頁
    Tagals of Luçon in the Philippines. As far as the little known respecting their language throws any light upon the subject, Mr. Swinhoe remarks that “some of the numerals and many other words, prove that they have a relation to the Malay,” and that “it is not improbable that that relation would be found stronger, if we could compare them with the intermediate tribes of the Philippine Islands.”     To proceed to the Mammals of Formosa, Mr. Swinhoe has collected what he has to say on this subject into a communication made to the Zoological Society in December last, in which he enumerates eighteen species as having come to his knowledge at that period. On them he makes the following general observations :—     “In my present article, I have been enabled to bring before the Society eighteen mammals from the island of Formosa. All of these, with the exception of the Hogdeer and the Hare, are mountain animals, and consequently of a mountain type, -- those that are identical with species found in China being generally darker and of more lively tints, and those that differ more resembling forms from the Himalayan Mountains than their representative species on the plains of China. It is, however, probable that, if we were better acquainted with the fauna of the hilly regions of the interior of China, we should find quite intermediate forms. On the whole, I am rather surprised that the isolation of the island has not tended to diversify animal forms more than it appears to have done. On the mountains of the interior, were they properly explored, we should doubtless discover more novelties: but the regions of the plains appear to agree, in flora as well as in fauna, almost entirely with the plains of the Chinese mainland. The Monkey is an animal frequenting the coast, and, in all probability, when we ascertain the rock-species found on some of the small groups of Chinese islands, we shall find them identical. The Sun-Bear is wondrously close to the Indian species, and, if the same, is sure to occur in some of the inner ranges of China. The Leopard is undoubtedly peculiar, and, I think, confined to the island. The Wild Cat and the Civet are certainly the Chinese species, the former having an extended range throughout tropical and semitropical Asia. The Paguma is a Chinese form, showing an insu- lar variation, incipient, so to speak, and scarcely strong enough in its distinctness to warrant specific separation. The Helictis and the Mole have felt the varying force of isolation and adaptation to peculiar circumstances, — the mountain-influences sequestrating the former from its Chinese ally, and making it affine to a cognate mountain species in the Himalayas.     “The Squirrels are so similar to the Himalayan species, that I cannot believe the result produced by climatal causes, and have little doubt that future research will bring to light their occurrence in the intermediate hilly and wooded ranges of China. Of the larger species, a close ally occurs in China, the Sciurus cinnamomeiventris ; but it strikes me, this latter animal is from the plain-country, and that the mountains will yet be found to yield the true Himalayan type, which occurs in Formosa, viz. the S. erythraeus. The two Flying Squirrels, we should expect to find distinct, as they vary in almost every island. The Pig I cannot speak about with certainty; but if a true Porcula, the fact would be greatly interesting, as more immediately connecting Formosa with the Himalayan chain. No Porcula is known to occur in China; but there is no reason why it should not have existed in former times, and been destroyed by its larger congener, the Wild Boar (Sus scrofa) from the north. The Mountain-Deer are interesting as being the species that offer most variation from their cognate China forms; but this may arise through ignorance on our part with regard to the Wild Deer of China. There is certainly a strong resemblance between the Cervus taivanus and the smaller C. sika of Japan, and between the Deer -Goat of Formosa and the Capricornis crispa of Japan; and indeed these two species appear to offer the only resemblance between the fauna of these two countries. I should, however, judging by analogy, be rather inclined to derive both these Formosan Cervidae from allied species that did exist in past times, and perhaps do even now exist in the unexplored parts of the opposite mainland. Geologists are, I understand, strong in the belief that Formosa was originally connected with Japan on the north and the Philippines on the south; but if any evidence can be deduced from the fauna of a country, I think a comparison of the present list with the ‘ Fauna Japonica,’ as well as a glance at my series of Birds, Reptiles, &c., will sufficiently prove that a stronger connection exists with the mainland of China than with the islands of Japan. In viewing the recently formed and still forming plains of Formosa on the western side, one would expect to find Chinese types among the birds brought by migration, and among the mammals by introduction of the Chinese colonists. But where we should chiefly look for affinity, viz. to the living productions of the ancient moun-
##--SPLIT--##原件缺第35頁    tain-ranges of the interior, we are struck with their thorough similarity to congeneric Chinese forms, rather than to those of the neighbouring islands of the Philippines on the south, or the Japanese islands on the north. It is true that the resemblance or identity of these productions is more with the Himalayan forms; but no zoologist or botanist that has collected in the mountains about Foochow can have failed to be struck with the similarity of the plants, as well as the birds, of the interior mountain-ranges of China with those of the Himalayas. I do not speak so confidently of mammals, because these are generally so rare in China that their acquisition is difficult ; but I can see no reason why the same law should not hold good for them as for other productions of nature.”     The new species described by Mr. Swinhoe are Macacus cyclopis (apparently a local variety of M. rhesus, and M. assamensis) ; Leopardus brachyurus (a form of Felix macrocelis of the Malayan countries) ; Helictis subaurantiaca (allied to H. moschata of China, aud H. nipalensis of the Himalayas) ; Talpa insularis (a blind mole, allied to Talpa caeca ?) ; Pteromys grandis (a very fine and striking species, allied to Pt. melanotis of Borneo, and Pt. albiventris of Nepal) ; Sciuropterus kaleensis ; and Porcula taivana. The latter, if really existing, for Mr. Swinhoe's evidence on the subject is somewhat indistinct, will be of great interest, as giving us a second representative of the curious P. salvania of the Saul-forests of Nepal. Three other new animals discovered by Mr. Swinhoe and previously described, are likewise important additions to our lists of recent Mammals. These are Capricornis swinhoii, Gray, belonging to the Asiatic form of Goat-like Antelopes, of which the Nepalese Thár (C. bubalina) is the best known species, and two Deer, Cervus swinhoii, Sclater, and C. taivanus, Blyth. Of both the latter species, Mr. Swinhoe has sent home living male specimens, which may be seen in the Zoological Society's Gardens.     The Birds of Formosa—the group to which Mr. Swinhoe seems to have devoted most of his energies—we have been informed that he kept thirty attendants hard at work shooting, and six others skinning the specimens for a considerable number of months—form the subject of a long and valuable Memoir in the “Ibis,” the title of which has been given above. In this, Mr. Swinhoe furnishes us with an account of no less than 201 species of birds found in Formosa, among which are many very interesting new species. Sixteen of the most remarkable of these had been previously described by Mr. Gould at one of the meetings of the Zoological Society in December last,* from specimens obtained by Mr. Swinhoe, and are intended to form the subjects of illustration in one of the forthcoming numbers of Mr. Gould's great work on the Birds of Asia. Other less distinct and less noticeable forms are characterized by Mr. Swinhoe himself in the present paper, and very full details are given concerning the manners, habits, structure, and nidification of all the species observed in the Island.     As in the Mammals of Formosa, so with regard to the Birds, Mr. Swinhoe observes, “two facts appear fully patent—that the animals of the plains and low country are for the most part identical with Chinese species, whilst those from the interior are more of a Himalayan type, and in some cases, too similar to be separated.” On the whole, Mr. Swinhoe has discovered at least forty species and separable local forms of birds in Formosa, and amongst them, some very fine and strongly marked species, allied to Indian forms; such as Psaropholus ardens, Megalama nuchalis, and Euploeamus swinhoii.     Concerning the Reptiles of Formosa, Mr. Swinhoe has chosen the “Annals of Natural History,” as the organ for the publication of his researches, which have resulted in the discovery of fifteen species found in the Island. Amongst them, are two new Lizards and a new Snake, to all three of which Dr. Günther seems to have tacked the curious specific MS. name, “Swinhonis”—or is it not rather the printer who has made a mistake in this instance? Mr. Swinhoe's series of Reptiles embrace only five Tortoises, three Lizards, and six Snakes, and there is probably a good deal more to be done in this department of Zoology.     Finally, a list of 246 species of plants collected by Mr. Swinhoe in Formosa shows that our Botanical friends have also something to thank Mr. Swinhoe for. Mr. Swinhoe's specimens have been deposited in the Kew Herbarium, and thirteen of them are considered by the authorities there as “probably new species.”     In conclusion, we must cordially thank Mr. Swinhoe in the name of Science for the energy he has displayed in this investigation of 原文無    the Fauna and Flora of this heretofore unexplored country. It would be well if certain other members of Her Majesty's Consular service, whose opportunities are not less, would take the lesson thus set to them and endeavour to exert themselves in a somewhat similar manner.＿＿＿＿＿＿    * See P.Z. S. 1862, p. 280, “Description of Sixteen New Species of Birds from the Island of Formosa, collected by Robert Swinhoe, Esq., H.M. Vice-Consul at Formosa.”

##--SPLIT--##V. 史溫侯的福爾摩沙自然史（1.） 福爾摩沙島記事。作者羅伯特．史溫侯，倫敦動物學學會會員、英國地質學會院士、駐臺灣領事。倫敦，1863。私人出版。（2.） 福爾摩沙民族學評註。作者羅伯特．史溫侯。倫敦 1863。（私人出版）。（3.） 論中國福爾摩沙島的哺乳動物。作者羅伯特．史溫侯。《倫敦動物學會集刊》。1862。第347頁。（4.） 福爾摩沙島鳥類學。作者羅伯特．史溫侯。《英國鳥類科學期刊》。1863。第198頁至第250頁至第377頁（5.） 福爾摩沙島之爬蟲類名錄及部分物種記事，和關於Orthagorisous sp.的一些評論。作者羅伯特．史溫侯。《自然史年報與雜誌》1863。（6.） 福爾摩沙島植物誌。作者羅伯特．史溫侯。（私人出版）。兩年前，與中國南方遙遙相望的大島嶼福爾摩沙，對博物學家來說仍是個「未知的國度（terra incognita）」，對地理學家來說，差不多也是如此。1860年12月，英國領事館的羅伯特‧史溫侯先生被任命為「臺灣府（Taiwan foo）」的副領事，##--SPLIT--##臺灣府是島上最大的中國港口。參考史溫侯先生的各篇文章（如上面列出的這些文章），就會發現史溫侯先生自被任命以來，在很短的時間內，在對這片島嶼的理解上有了大幅的進展。扭轉了我們以往對福爾摩沙及其住民的一無所知的狀況，讓人們對這個研究主題能夠有還算完備的認識。史溫侯先生在短暫離開後又再次返回工作崗位，他肯定會繼續努力，完成這項由他開啟的重要任務。    史溫侯先生可說是這個研究主題的唯一權威，我們從他的著作中瞭解到，福爾摩沙位於中國沿海附近，幾乎呈南北走向，距離中國大陸約80英里。該島長約120英里，寬度從26到80英里不等，整個島嶼的面積和愛爾蘭差不多。島上三分之一是平地，其餘部分則是起伏的丘陵地區和山脈，其中一些山峰的高度超過12,000英呎，而且終年被積雪覆蓋。因此，就地理結構來說，福爾摩沙和我們剛剛用來比較的愛爾蘭截然不同。從動物學的觀點來看，有更多不同之處。因為近代才從歐洲分離出來的愛爾蘭，幾乎沒有幾種（如果有也不多）在鄰近歐洲大陸上不存在的物種。我們現在要說明，福爾摩沙已經從中國大陸分離了好幾個世紀，在動物學上發生了明顯的變化，已經成為一些非常獨特、具代表性之物種形態的發源地。    首先我們來談一談福爾摩沙的民族學。大致來說，這個島嶼及其附屬地居住著兩個族群，分別是馬來族的原住民（aboriginal savages）和蒙古族的中國墾殖者（Chinese colonists）。中國墾殖者（Chinese）主要佔領島嶼的西半部，與中國沿海相望的平原地區。而原住民（aborigines）的居住範圍則侷限於島嶼東半部的山區。僅有後者，才具有民族學上的意義，福爾摩沙似乎有五個部族（tribes）：即南部山區的傀儡番（Kales）、北部山區的奎輝社（Kweiyings）、東北部山區的太魯閣番（Tylokos）、東北部平原的噶瑪蘭番（Komalans）、還有新港（Sinkang）的平埔番（Pepos）。最後一族幾乎完全與中國人同化，而倒數第二個部族正在快速消失。第六個部族是赤崁番（Sakkam），似乎已經完全滅絕了。史溫侯先生的報告，提供了他手邊所有關於這些部族的資訊。這些部族可能是四處漂泊的馬來人，與菲律賓呂宋島（Luçon）的他加祿人（Tagals）有共同的血緣。至於他們鮮為人知的語言進一步說明了哪些事情，史溫侯先生指出，「一些數字和許多其他字詞，證明了他們與馬來人有關係」，    ##--SPLIT--##原件缺第33頁
    而且「如果我們可以將他們與菲律賓群島的中間部落（intermediate tribes）做比較，我們有可能會發現他們之間的關係比想像中還緊密。」    為了進入到《福爾摩沙的哺乳類動物（The Mammals of Formosa）》的討論，史溫侯先生已將他在這個主題上的看法整理在去年12月寄給皇家動物學會的一份文件裡，其中列出了當時他所知的18種哺乳類動物。對這些物種，史溫侯先生提出了以下概論：「在我這篇文章裡，我要將福爾摩沙島的18種哺乳動物介紹給皇家動物學會。除了鹿（Hogdeer）和野兔（Hare），其他都是山區的動物，因而一致為山區型：牠們與在中國發現的物種相同，通常外觀為暗淡且蒼白不飽和，比起中國平地上的典型物種，牠們更像是源自於喜馬拉雅山脈的物種。但是，如果我們對中國內陸丘陵地區的動物相有更多瞭解，則很有可能找到介於兩者之間的中間形態。整體來說，令我感到相當驚訝的是，「（地理）隔離（效應）並沒有讓這個島嶼（福爾摩沙）上的動物外形變化如同預期中的多樣。如果我們對內山好好地進行調查，毫無疑問地會有更多新發現。但是，就平地來說，似乎在植物相和動物相方面，幾乎都與中國大陸的平地相同。猴子是一種經常在沿海地區出沒的動物，當我們能夠確定一些中國小島上發現的岩棲型猴子的種類，我們很可能會發現牠們是一樣的物種。馬來熊1與印度的物種具有驚人的相似性，如果是相同物種的話，肯定會在中國的一些內陸地區發現牠們的蹤跡。豹，毫無疑問是非常特殊的物種，而且我認為，牠僅分布於福爾摩沙。石虎和白鼻心（Civet）肯定是中國的物種，前者的活動範圍遍及整個亞洲的熱帶和亞熱帶地區。白鼻心（Paguma）是中國的一種物種，身上已展現出某種獨特的島內變異，不過可以說仍在萌芽階段，其特徵還沒有顯著到讓我們有理由將牠視為一個獨立的物種。鼬獾（Helictis）2和鼴鼠則受到地理隔離和對特殊環境的適應性的影響，也就是山的影響力，將前者從其中國的近緣物種中分離出來，使其與其喜馬拉雅山區的同類物種更加相似。」    「松鼠與喜馬拉雅山的物種非常相似，我不敢相信這是氣候造成的結果，並且毫無疑問，未來的研究將證明同樣的物種也出現在中國中部丘陵和森林繁茂的地區。以較大型的種類來說，在中國有一種近緣物種，是紅腹松鼠（Sciurus cinnamomeiventris，目前學名 Callosciurus erythraeus）；但我認為，紅腹松鼠來自平原地區，而在山區還生長著一種真正喜馬拉雅山型的松鼠，也就是出現在福爾摩沙的赤腹松鼠（S. erythraeus，目前學名 Callosciurus erythraeus）。3關於那兩種飛鼠，我們應該會找到截然不同的種類，因為牠們在每個島上幾乎都有所不同。關於豬的部分，我還無法確定；但如果是真正的姬豬（Porcula），整件事就非常有趣了，因為牠更直接地將福爾摩沙與喜馬拉雅山脈連結起來。目前已知中國沒有姬豬；但是有可能過去曾存在過，且被來自北方、更大的同類野豬（Sus scrofa）所消滅。4山區的鹿很有趣，牠們與中國的同類動物之間有最多變異。但這可能是我們對中國的野鹿不夠瞭解所致。臺灣梅花鹿（Cervus taivanus，目前學名 Cervus nippon taiouanus）和日本較小的日本梅花鹿（Cervus sika，目前學名 Cervus nippon）之間；以及臺灣野山羊（Deer-Goat）5與日本髭羚（Capricornis crispa，目前學名Capricornis crispus）之間，無疑有很高的相似性。確實，這兩個物種似乎是兩國的動物相之間唯一的相似之處。但是，用類推來判斷的話，我比較傾向認為這兩種福爾摩沙的鹿科動物皆起緣於過去確實存在，而且甚至現在也可以在對岸的未探索地區找到的近緣物種。據我所知，地質學家堅信福爾摩沙最早北與日本相連，南與菲律賓相接。但是，如果可以從一個國家的動物相中找出任何蛛絲馬跡，我認為將目前的名錄與「日本動物誌（Fauna Japonica）」進行比較，並且再瀏覽一下我關於鳥類、爬蟲類等的系列文章，就足以證明福爾摩沙的物種與中國大陸有更密切的關係，而不是日本列島。在考察最近剛於福爾摩沙西岸形成並仍在持續擴張的平原時，我們應該會在遷徙而來的鳥類以及中國墾殖者引入的哺乳動物中，找到具中國形態的個體。

---------------1 譯註：當時可能認為臺灣黑熊是馬來熊（Helarctos malayanus）。2 譯註：臺灣的鼬獾在當時史溫侯所採用的學名為Helictis subaurantiaca，命名文獻為Swinhoe, Robert. 1863. On the mammals of the island of Formosa (China). Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London 1862: 347-365. 目前學名為Melogale moschata，亞種名為subaurantiaca。3 譯註：當時認為中國和台灣的赤腹松鼠為不同物種，現在認為相同物種Callosciurus erythraeus。為了在譯文中作區別，分別採用中國慣用的中文俗名和台灣慣用的中文俗名。4 譯註：有關台灣的野豬和姬豬的分類，依循 Burgin CJ. 2020. Illustrated Checklist of the Mammals of the World, Volume 1 & 2. Lynx Edicions 處理。台灣野豬尚歸屬於歐亞野豬(Sus scrofa)的亞種(S. s. taivanus)，而姬豬屬(Porcula sp.)目前全世界僅有一屬一種「姬豬(Porcula salvania)」，侷限分布於尼泊爾及孟加拉交界、雅魯藏布江附近。5 譯註：目前台灣野山羊歸屬於牛科。
##--SPLIT--##原件缺第35頁        我們應該首先尋找牠們跟古老內山生物之間的親緣性，但在這些物種身上，我們感覺到牠們與中國的同類物種完全相似，而非南部的菲律賓群島或北部的日本列島的物種。的確，這些生物與喜馬拉雅山形態的生物有更多相似之處或更高的同質性。但是，沒有任何一個在福州（Foochow）附近山區採集的動物學家或植物學家，不會對中國內陸山脈的植物和鳥類與喜馬拉雅山脈形態之間的相似程度感到震驚。我對談哺乳動物沒那麼有信心，因為一般來說哺乳動物在中國相當罕見，以至於很難取得牠們的標本。但我看不出有什麼理由，大自然中其他生物所依循的的自然法則不應該同樣適用於牠們身上。」    史溫侯先生描述的新物種是臺灣獼猴（Macacus cyclopis，目前學名 Macaca cyclopis）（顯然是恒河猴（Macacus rhesu，目前學名Macacus mulattarhesu）和熊猴（Macacus assamensis）的在地變種）。 臺灣雲豹（Leopardus brachyurus，目前學名Neofelis nebulosa brachyura）（屬於馬來地區國家的雲豹（Felix macrocelis，目前學名Neofelis diardi）的一種形態）；鼬獾（Helicits subaurantiaca，目前學名Melogale moschata subaurantiaca）（與中國的中國鼬獾（H. moschata，目前學名 Melogale moschata）和喜馬拉雅山的緬甸鼬獾（H. nipalensis，目前學名Melogale personata）有親緣關係）；臺灣鼴鼠（Talpa insularis，目前學名 Mogera insularis insularis）（一種喪失視覺的鼴鼠，與地中海鼴鼠（Talpa caeca）有親緣關係）；大赤鼯鼠（Pteromys grandis，目前學名Petaurista philippensis grandis）（非常優良且令人驚艷的物種，與婆羅洲的棕鼯鼠（Pteromys melanotis，目前學名Petaurista petaurista）和尼泊爾的棕鼯鼠南亞亞種（Pt. albiventris，目前學名Petaurista petaurista albiventer）有親緣關係）；小鼯鼠（Sciuropterus kaleensis，目前學名Belomys pearsonii kaleensis）和臺灣野豬（Porcula taivana，目前學名 Sus scrofa taivanus）。由於史溫侯先生在這個主題上的證據較為薄弱，後者如果確實存在，將具有重大的意義，因為這代表在這裡尼泊爾撒烏耳森林的稀奇姬豬（P. salvania） 又多了第二個典型代表。 另外還有三種由史溫侯先生發現且之前描述過的新物種，也是近期哺乳類動物名錄中的重要補充。這3種包括臺灣野山羊（Capricornis swinhoii，目前學名Capricornis swinhoei），體色為灰色，屬於亞洲形態的羚羊，是種類似山羊的羚羊。其中以尼泊爾的塔爾羊（Capricornis bubalina）是最廣為人知的物種。還有兩種鹿，水鹿（Cervus swinhoii, Sclater，目前學名 Cervus unicolor swinhoii）和臺灣梅花鹿（C. taivanus, Blyth，目前學名 Cervus nippon taiouanus）。史溫侯先生將後面兩種的雄性活體都已經寄回英國，可以在皇家動物學會的庭院中看到。    史溫侯先生似乎投入了大部分心力在福爾摩沙島的鳥類上，據了解，他請了30名隨從專門負責採集標本的工作，另外六名隨從則是花了好幾個月製作剝製標本，完成了《英國鳥類科學期刊》當中的寶貴的長篇專題論文，文章標題如前文所示。在這篇文章裡，史溫侯先生為我們介紹了至少201種福爾摩沙的鳥類，其中有許多非常有趣的新鳥種。顧爾德先生（Mr. Gould）去年12月在皇家動物學會的會議中描述了其中最特別的16種：取自史溫侯先生採集的標本，預定作為古爾德先生即將出版的一系列偉大著作之一「亞洲的鳥類（The Birds of Asia）」中插圖的題材。 史溫侯先生本人在文中還描述了其他一些較不獨特和不太引人注意的鳥類，並提供了所有在福爾摩沙島上觀察到的鳥種的型態、習性、身體構造和築巢方式等非常詳細的資訊。    史溫侯先生提到，和福爾摩沙島的哺乳動物一樣，就鳥類來說，「有兩個相當明顯的現象：棲息在平原和低地的物種大多與中國的物種相同，而內陸的物種則比較像是喜馬拉雅山的物種。有些案例因為太相似而無法區分。」整體來說，史溫侯先生在福爾摩沙發現了至少40種鳥種以及一些獨立的原生鳥類形態，其中包括一些與印度的鳥類有親緣關係，非常美麗且特徵明顯的鳥類。 如朱鸝（Psaropholus ardens，目前學名 Oriolus traillii）、臺灣擬啄木（Megalama nuchalis，目前學名 Pslopogon nuchalis）和藍腹鷴（Euplocamus swinhoii，目前學名 Lophura swinhoii）。    關於福爾摩沙島的爬蟲類，史溫侯先生選擇了在《自然史年鑑》上發表其研究成果，他最終在島上發現了15種物種。 其中包括兩種新種的蜥蜴和一種新種的蛇類，貢德博士（Dr. Günther）6似乎給這3種新種都加上了奇怪的「史溫侯（Swinhonis）」字樣，還是說這是印刷錯誤？史溫侯先生所列的爬蟲類只包含五種龜鱉類，3種蜥蜴類和6種蛇類，看來在動物學的部門可能還有很多事情要做。    最後，我們植物學界的朋友也要感謝史溫侯先生，他整理了福爾摩沙246種植物的名錄。這些植物標本已經保存於英國皇家博物館，其中有13個物種，福爾摩沙當局認為可能是新物種。在最後，我們科學界必須衷心感謝史溫侯先生在調查這個迄今尚未被探索的土地的動物相和植物相中，所付出的心力。如果領事館的其他人員，可以汲取他們的經驗，並努力以類似的方式發揮自己的長才，那會是美事一樁。
---------------6 譯註：全名Albert Karl Ludwig Gotthilf Günther，為德國裔英國動物學家，1857年就職於大英博物館，專攻魚類學，1865年獲選進入英國皇家學會。

plans.name: 19世紀西方台灣書寫史料翻譯整理計畫
plans.description:
本計畫翻譯法文史料與英文史料。法文史料《法軍遠征福爾摩沙1884-1885》作者手稿及其附錄《法軍遠征福爾摩沙1884-1885 》地圖手繪稿，為法國軍官嘉諾（Eugène Germain Garnot）紀錄清法戰爭期間在雞籠、淡水、澎湖等地的見聞。此書為清法戰爭現存最詳盡的西文資料，1894年曾於法國出版，1960年由臺灣銀行經濟研究室出版中譯本《法軍侵台始末》，是研究清法戰爭臺灣之役的重要參考。此外，英文史料為臺灣自然史相關論文30篇，是英國博物學家史溫侯（Robert Swinhoe, 1836-1877，亦翻譯為郇和、斯文豪、士委諾）在19世紀中葉擔任英國駐臺領事期間所撰寫的，紀錄臺灣的自然生態與物產，是當時西方世界了解臺灣的重要資料。另外本案包含史溫侯所撰臺灣相關文章清單1份，是費德廉老師所整理的。

完成年份：2020年

計畫執行團隊
內容力有限公司
